data.title: Pagaya Technologies: Yes, It's Been Ugly -- But The Long-Term Case Remains Intact
data.description:
Quite frankly, my bullish take on Pagaya Technologies (PGY) was my worst call of 2025. I didn't expect the market to reprice the software stocks to such a negative extent. Also, PGY's price action was terrible. Every rally was sold off despite the fintech posting top and bottom-line growth.

Yes, the downtrend was brutal, but it looks like the $10 support level has held.

So, is my bullish thesis over? No, I don't think so. I personally believe that the bearish AI narrative seems overblown. The fintech trades at 4.5x forward P/E now, and for quite some time was below $1 billion in market cap. Ouch. But it also makes me think that the risk to reward ratio appears appealing at this price tag.

I'd love to point out that I see bullish catalysts developing favorably for Pagaya. That's why I am not changing my bullish rating. I still think it's a Strong Buy. And here's why.

Cautious Guidance Resulted In 30% Drop

The fintech had earnings a few months ago, and the stock dropped about 20% since. The way I see it, two main catalysts drove the sentiment down.

Surely enough, it didn't help that PGY delivered lower than analysts anticipated revenue growth. But I think what actually moved the needle was management's commentary on reduced risk going for 2026.

I honestly love that the company said they reduced exposure to high risk. Of course, that will impact bottom-line growth over the next year, but it should also make the business more sustainable. I honestly don't think that's bad for the long-term. We have risks tied to the possibility of inflation rebound due to high oil prices in the short-term, which could consider the FED raising rates. As a result, consumers may be pressured further. So, I believe it's mature for the company to proceed with a safer approach. And I also would love to point out management sees the market and trends of consumer health, so to say, from the inside. I trust that. If PGY believes there is no need to take extra risk in a short-term, I am fine with that. On top of this, PGY still sees network volume growth for the next year. So, at this 4.5x earnings multiple to me it looks like heavily undervalued. And this repricing downward due to this cautious guidance.. Well, I personally don't see this as justifiable.

Next Earnings May Set a Tone

Pagaya is expected to share quarterly results in a few weeks. Now, the market expects $0.56 in diluted EPS (18% decline YoY). And $323 million in revenue (11% growth YoY). Quite honestly, given the recent drop in the stock price, I think that negative news is largely priced in. So, if PGY announces at least in-line with the expectations, I wouldn't be surprised by a positive stock price reaction.

But could it happen that the stock declines further after the earnings? Absolutely. If they decide to reduce exposure to risk even more, there's a chance both of the metrics come in weak. Now, that's surely not my base case, but that's what I will be looking for the most in this report. If management comments that they are not willing to pull back on exposure even more, this could signal a more positive shift in outlook. I also want to see FRLPC margin come in within the expected range of 4% to 5%. This could calm down investors and ignite a turnaround from the current levels.

This report may set a tone for the remainder of 2026 for PGY. I am looking forward to it.

At 4.5x Forward P/E, AI Threat Is Priced In

Yes, that's right. I don't think you can reprice it significantly lower. Even if AI threats remain as a key concern and competition intensifies, it may still be quite difficult to reprice it lower. Especially, if PGY continues growing at double-digit pace.

Now, with network volume growth expectations, it tells me clients favor PGY services and they are happy with it. On top of this, PGY is a disruptor itself, modernizing credit landscape. So, even if new companies enter the market, I don't think PGY will sit on their hands. That's why this fear seems to be overblown.

I honestly love this chart below comparing PGY and tech-software ETF (IGV) price action. To me it seems that PGY followed IGV, but acted as a higher beta stock, resulting in more severe volatility.

Quite honestly, I wouldn't be surprised if a few good quarters exceeding expectations could completely change the bearish narrative for PGY. At least indicate that the fintech could be at the forefront of the AI revolution in the credit space. And that's not that likely it will be disrupted by competition. That's actually one of the things I will be also looking forward to seeing if management communicates about further AI adoption in the following earnings call.

New FED Chairman May Ignite Risk on Rally

Now, I know I have pointed out inflation rebound as potential threat. But quite frankly, that's not my base case.

The FED is going to have a new chairman next month. This is Trump's appointed professional, and we know that the President was extremely vocal about cutting interest rates. Now, Kevin Warsh has already shared his agenda, and he sees interest rate cuts in 2026. Interestingly enough, the market is pricing no cut in 2026 as the base case with roughly 70%.

In my honest opinion, this is where the market underestimates the FED policy. I wouldn't be surprised if we seen at least a cut by the end of this year. And if that were to happen, risk on assets could rally. This supports my bullish thesis for Pagaya.

Pagaya Remains Undervalued with Solid Margins

Well, the chart below shows that at roughly 4.5x forward P/E, PGY is valued at multi-year low valuation. If that weren't enough, it trades at an 80% discount versus the sector median at approximately 22.5x forward P/E. Honestly, I think that's a significant difference. And if the catalysts were to play out positively, re-pricing could come next.

Pagaya trades at 0.70 forward Price/Sales, nearly 77% below the industry's peers at 3.00. To me it tells that the market hasn't priced in any future growth. So, when I pointed out that if PGY reports better than analysts expected, it could result in positive stock price action. The fintech also trades at forward EV/EBITDA of 1.92, which is about 86% below the sector median of 13.41. I believe this supports my thesis that risk to reward seems appealing at the current levels, but, surely, time will tell.

Now, I'd also love to point out that PGY has low leverage capital structure. Cash of $1.18 billion exceeds its total obligations. It's not something you often see at single digit valuation.

I'd also like to note that Pagaya is growing revenue at significantly higher pace than peers. Over the past 12 months its top-line surged by 26%, a 2.6x outperformance versus the sector median at 10%. To me it tells it deserves a higher premium. Also, that the sell-off fueled by AI threat and IGV downward pressure could seem overblown.

Pagaya operates at above the industry's average net income margin (TTM) of 6.25%, a 20% outperformance versus peers at 5.21%. Clearly the fundamentals are strong. I don't think it's a bad business. But, yes, the stock's price action has been difficult lately.

Why The Bullish Case May Not Play Out?

Absolutely there is a scenario where my bullish thesis doesn't play out. I'd love to point out a few things that could negatively impact stock price in the short-term.

Now as I already stated above, macroeconomic concerns may impact investor appetite. Well, the way I see it is that if the conflict in the Middle East persists, there's a possibility of inflation rebound. Even though I don't really think the new FED could consider hiking rates, but it may prolong the cutting cycle. If that were to happen, surely enough, risk-on assets are likely to remain suppressed. This wouldn't be great for PGY, too.

Higher rates for longer wouldn't be an ideal scenario for consumers, as well. If both sentiment and spending reduce, this could signal strong deterioration of the economy. This could lead to more credit defaults. One thing I'd love to point out here is that maybe PGY's risk reduction could come timely here, but obviously time will tell.

There's also a possibility that AI-related threats continue weighing on the stock. But I don't think that's a base case. The way I see it, PGY is an AI driven company itself, and I don't think it will sit on its hands. But if the broader market sells off due to this, PGY stock could be impacted.

Lastly, as PGY experienced massive sell-off, we should understand that the market may not be willing to give a significant premium for quite some time. I honestly wouldn't be surprised if investors wanted to wait a quarter or two and monitor the performance. So, there's surely an opportunity cost risk that is valid.

I Don't Minimize Risks, But I Also Stay Rational

Sure enough, Pagaya's recent stock price action was devastating. But I don't think it's time to throw out the towel just yet. The fintech still has strong fundamentals, and at the current price tag it looks undervalued.

Of course, it may take some time for the market to reprice it. But if PGY continues double-digit revenue growth, I wouldn't be surprised if it happens sooner, rather than later. As I pointed out above, there are also other catalysts that could move the stock. The company's cautious approach to me seems like a mature move and the sell-off looks overblown. Historically, the risk to reward ratio at this valuation resulted in a turnaround. Of course, this is not guaranteed. Let's see what happens next.

I maintained my rating as a Strong Buy. My opinion resonates well with Wall Street analysts. They forecast a $26.45 price target, suggesting 103% upside possibility.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: Monthly Pay With Strong Results
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

Gladstone Investment (GAIN) is a business development company ("BDC") that has been delivering solid results through an attractive monthly dividend to our portfolio. This dividend has been incredibly steady since its public launch, cutting only once during the Global Financial Crisis. So it may not be able to boast as strongly as Main Street Capital (MAIN) as having never cut, but it is an accomplishment nonetheless. There have also been a number of sizeable supplementals paid out to investors as well from GAIN, thanks to strong gains from its underlying portfolio.

GAIN is a bit unique in that they are a much more concentrated portfolio, and have a sizeable allocation to equity positions. That can increase overall risks for this name relative to senior loan-focused BDCs that have significant diversification across hundreds of outstanding loans, but it can come with some higher rewards as well.

GAIN Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.79
  • Discount/Premium: -2.81%
  • Dividend Yield: 6.55%
  • Total Expense Ratio: 17.12%
  • Leverage Ratio: 0.97x
  • Managed Assets: $1.237 billion

GAIN is a BDC that "makes combined equity and EBT investments in established private businesses in the U.S., in connection with buyouts or other recapitalizations of those companies." They generally target a portfolio over time of "approximately 75% in debt securities and 25% in equity securities, at cost."

Management Shake-Up

In some recent news for GAIN, they announced that David Gladstone will be stepping down as the CEO immediately. However, he'll still remain on as the Chairman of the Board. As some may already know, David Gladstone is the founder of Gladstone Management Corporation, the advisor to this BDC and several other investment solutions. This is pretty big news, then that he will be stepping back from the day-to-day operations. However, long-time veterans of the company will be taking over.

David Dullum, the Company's President since 2008, has succeeded Mr. Gladstone as CEO, and John Sateri, a twenty-year veteran at Gladstone and a current member of the Company's investment committee, has been appointed Chief Investment Officer.

Erika Highland and Christopher Lee, both current senior managing directors, have also been appointed Executive Vice Presidents of the Company, effective immediately. After over twenty years at Gladstone in various roles of increasing responsibility, Ms. Highland will also succeed Mr. Dullum as the Company's President, effective October 1, 2026.

Dullum and Lee have been with Gladstone since 2008, and Erika Highland joined in 2005. Suffice it to say, it seems like this isn't too much of a worry at all, with the executive team being well-seasoned and quite familiar with operations.

Strong Performance With Concentrated Exposure

GAIN has delivered solid total returns since our last focus write-up. A period where the BDC was at a rather attractive valuation, going back to September 2024.

In looking at the last time we highlighted this name as a potential opportunity, it was back in April 2025. At that time, we were also highlighting Trinity Capital (TRIN) as an interesting opportunity at an attractive valuation. During that specific period, we were in the sell-off induced by the Trump administration announcing retaliatory tariffs. Since that time, GAIN has also delivered solid total returns. TRIN also did during this period, but it gave back quite a bit of the gains it had seen at the start of 2026.

Today, the valuation isn't as appealing as it was back in each of those times, though it is still trading at a slight discount to its last reported net asset value.

That said, relative to other BDCs, its share price hasn't been as pressured, but this is actually probably a positive. It is because it is a differentiated approach, and that has led to the portfolio holding up relatively well. While most BDCs are seeing NAV decreases and increasing non-accruals, GAIN hasn't been seeing that sort of weakness.

In fact, the NAV per share climbed $1.42 in the last quarter, coming in at $14.95 compared to $13.53 in the prior quarter. This was driven by a signficant $1.77 in unrealized appreciation and $0.09 realized gains. Though it was then partially offset due to the distributions and net investment loss experienced, which combined was $0.19 in that quarter.

That is a significant factor that can help highlight the volatility that can come with having such a high allocation to equity investments. In this case, it was positive upside volatility, at least for those long the shares. The latest shows that around 43% of the investment portfolio is allocated to the preferred/common equity sleeve.

That still leaves a sizeable 49% of first lien exposure, but a typical BDC that one thinks of generally has a meaningfully higher allocation to loans. Additionally, the portfolio is comprised of only 29 companies. This is well below something like popular Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and its 603 portfolio companies—primarily allocated to loan investments.

On the nonaccrual front, this is three companies, and they are looking at 3.8% of the portfolio at cost and 1.5% at fair value.

Monthly Pay

As noted at the opening, GAIN comes with a solid monthly payout, delivering significant supplementals when those equity investments really pay off with big wins.

That doesn't guarantee that it will continue to happen in the future, but the regular monthly payout does look like it is in solid shape. It isn't always entirely covered by the net investment income, but it does come in close. The latest adjusted NII in their last quarterly report came in a bit shy at $0.21— which was down from the $0.24 in the prior quarter.

Given they still have a meaningful sleeve of senior loan exposure primarily based on floating rates, they aren't entirely immune to interest rate changes. Given that the Fed had been cutting its target rate, this was expected to put pressure on underlying income-generation. At the same time, it is only a small shortfall, which can and was covered by capital gains elsewhere.

On that same front, similar to the more traditional BDCs, their form of leverage comes in several different instruments. That includes senior notes, which often come with fixed-rate financing and account for a sizable portion of GAIN's outstanding debt.

In February, after this report, they announced a new publicly offering of notes that trade under (GAING), coming with a 7.125% rate. They were looking to repay a portion of their outstanding credit facility at the time—though left in the usual boilplate flexibility that it could also be "to fund new investment opportunities, and for other general corporate purposes."

At the time of the last report, that would put the debt-to-equity ratio at 0.97x, though it would be a bit higher if they took the new $100 raised from GAING and did not pay down the credit facility. Either way, this is on the more conservative side, but that is more appropriate given the higher concentration of the portfolio and equity exposure.

Conclusion

GAIN isn't perhaps at a great valuation like we saw in our last two times highlighting this name, primarily because while most of its BDC cousins are in a slump, GAIN is not. It still is trading at a bit of a discount to its net asset value, but that's actually thanks to a strong uptick in NAV per share during the last report. That is going in the opposite direction of most other BDCs that are lucky to hold NAV about flat in recent reporting, so this overall is a positive for GAIN as it is holding up relatively well. Additionally, GAIN has a solid long-term track record and can be an appropriate holding for investors who can take on some greater risk. The monthly dividend it pays makes it easy to hold over the long term for an income-focused investor as well.

data.title: Nektar Therapeutics Upcoming AA Data Prediction: Why I Think It Will Be Positive
data.description:
Previously in my article titled "Nektar Therapeutics: Unique MOA Translates To Stellar AD Maintenance Data, Strong Buy", I delved into Nektar's main drug called REZPEG, and its maintenance data in P2b portion of the atopic dermatitis ((AD)) trial, and the data was very positive. For more background information on the company and REZPEG mechanism of action, please refer to the previous article.

Here I will discuss another key catalyst for NKTR that is coming up before the end of this April: REZPEG maintenance data in Alopecia Areata (AA) P2B trial called REZOLVE-AA. In short, based on the detailed 36wk induction data disclosed previously, plus the proven deepening response of REZPEG from the AD trial, I believe the data from the P2B 52wk maintenance period of the AA trial will very likely be positive.

Specifically, my model suggests that by the end of 52wk maintenance period, REZPEG will show a probability-adjusted 28% patients achieving SALT score <20 in the treatment arms, which is the gold standard for measuring effective hair growth in AA. This data will match or exceed the current standard of care which is low dose (2mg) Baricitinib at 52 wk showing ~23% patients with SALT score <20. If this happens, there is high probability that NKTR's share will soar 30-40%, given as of now REZPEG's use case in AA is barely included in the valuation due to the controversial 36wk induction data, as I will explain below.

REZPEG for AA: 36wk induction data overview

The 36-week induction data was controversial, to say the least, primarily because four patients with major eligibility violations were mistakenly enrolled in the trial. This error became the make-or-break factor for the primary endpoint: the percentage of SALT score reduction. This selection of primary endpoint is also strange since it is a lot less strict than what most AA trials use which is %patients that reach <SALT20.

Essentially, if those four patients are included, the placebo (PBO) SALT reduction percentage increases, causing the p-value between the treatment arms and the placebo to exceed 0.05. If those patients are excluded, the p-value remains below 0.05, reaching statistical significance.

At the very least, this type of execution error reflects poorly on management and was, understandably, not well-received by investors. It makes the data interpretation murky; however, the numerical trend remains notable. Regardless of whether those four patients are included, there is an overall LS mean reduction in SALT score of approximately 30%, which is clinically meaningful.

Looking at the more commonly used endpoint for AA, which is %patient with <SALT 20, we see about 15% in each of the treatment arms. This is slightly worse than the 2mg Baricitinib, which scored ~19.5% for <SALT20 on average across two P3 trials. Given REZPEG is not a JAK inhibitor like Baricitinib and has much more benign side effects and likely no black box warning, a small drop in efficacy here is acceptable.

REZPEG for AA: 52wk maintenance data prediction

While the 36-week induction data appears neutral to slightly bearish—exacerbated by the inclusion of patients who violated the entry criteria—there is a compelling case for a bullish outlook on the 52-week maintenance data. This optimism is based on the trajectory shown in the graph below.

To evaluate the potential for success, we must track individual patient progress toward the <SALT20 threshold:

  • The Baseline: With an average enrollment SALT score of 78 , patients require an average reduction of 74% to reach the <SALT20 target.

  • The Cohorts: Each blue bar represents an individual patient treated with REZPEG. Numbering them from right to left (by efficacy), Patients 1 through 10 have already achieved <SALT20 during the induction phase.

  • The Maintenance Phase: The "green dots" represent patients who completed the 36-week induction and enrolled in the 16-week extension, totaling 52 weeks of treatment.

The goal is to project the outcomes for Patients 11 through 25. By analyzing the "green dots" within this specific group, we can estimate how many will bridge the gap to a >74% SALT reduction by the end of the maintenance period.

If a significant portion of these mid-range responders continues to improve, the 52-week data could outperform current market expectations.

To make this easier, here is the breakdown of 3 scenarios: Bear case, base case, and bull case.

Patient # (toal # of patients) SALT % change (at data cutoff) <SALT20Bear case <SALT20Base case <SALT20Bull case
1 to 10 (10 total) 80-100% 10 10 10
11 to 14 (4 total) 68-80% 4 4 4
15 to 21 (7 total, 5 green ) 58-68% 1 2 5
22-26 (5 total, 4 green) 45-55% 0 2 3
25+ (42 total) 45-55% 0 1 1
Total # of patients with <SALT20 15 19 23
Total # of patients in treatment arms 67 67 67
%Patients that achieved <SALT20 22% 28% 34%

Patients 1–10 (Total: 10): We have established that these 10 patients likely achieved <SALT20 during the 36-week treatment; therefore, we have assigned 10 successes to every scenario.

Patients 11–14 (Total: 4): NKTR reported that at the time of the data cutoff, three additional patients had achieved <SALT20, while two were still in ongoing treatment. I have included Patient #12 (indicated by the purple dot) as also achieving <SALT20; given their 75% SALT reduction, this is a safe assumption. This results in 4 successes for every scenario.

Patients 15–21 (Total: 7): At this point, the three scenarios begin to diverge. Since these seven patients already show a SALT reduction of 58–68%, it is reasonable to assume more than half will reach a 74% reduction (and thus <SALT20) by week 52. Consequently, for the Base Case, I assigned 4 patients to reach <SALT20 by week 52.

Patients 22–25 (Total: 4): Because these four patients currently have a SALT reduction of 45–55%, the Base Case scenario assumes half (2 patients) will reach <SALT20 by week 52.

Remaining Patients: To remain conservative, we assume only one additional patient reaches <SALT20 in the Base Case. By assigning probabilities of 20% to the Bear Case, 60% to the Base Case, and 20% to the Bull Case, the probability-adjusted <SALT20 rate is:

(22%×20%)+(28%×60%)+(34%×20%)=28%

Comparison to Phase 3 Baricitinib Data above in Figure 1 at Week 52:

  • Baricitinib 2mg: 22.8% achieved <SALT20

  • Baricitinib 4mg: 38.9% achieved <SALT20

REZPEG was benchmarked to 2mg Baricitinib, so at the projected probability-adjusted 28% comparing to Baricitinib's 23%, the margin of safety for the upcoming REZPEG AA data is large.

Potential risks

The main risk for the thesis is REZPEG works too slowly to have a significant effect for these AA patients in the maintenance portion. However, due to stellar AD maintenance data and proven deepening response in that trial, as well as the fact that even in bear case I calculated above we have 22% patients reaching <SALT20, this risk is manageable.

The other risk is unexpected safety signal. Again this is very unlikely because REZPEG has been dosed in multiple trials and the safety signals have been consistent, with the most common adverse event being injection site reactions, which in most cases are mild and disappear in a few days.

Financial health and recent offering

As mentioned in my previous article, Nektar Therapeutics recently bolstered its balance sheet through a substantial $460 million public offering, issuing over 7.6 million shares at $58.00 per share. Although this resulted in roughly 30–35% dilution for current shareholders, the move was viewed as a net positive, extending the company's cash runway into 2027 and beyond.

NKTR Valuation if AA maintenance trial succeeds

The Total Addressable Market (TAM) for Alopecia Areata ((AA)) is projected to reach approximately $6B by 2030. Assuming REZPEG has a 50% probability of approval and achieves 20% peak market penetration, it would reach a risk-adjusted peak revenue of $600M. Applying a conservative Price-to-Sales (P/S) ratio of 2, the AA indication alone could be valued at $1.2B. Given the current negative sentiment surrounding Nektar’s (NKTR) AA induction data, the company's $2.3B market cap does not appear to materially price in success for this indication.

Consequently, if the Phase 2b AA maintenance trial succeeds and meets this base case—which would bode very well for the Phase 3 trial—an additional $0.8B–$1B should be added to NKTR’s valuation. This implies a 30–40% increase in share price, moving from the current $80.50 to approximately $100–$110.

data.title: Bank Of America: Fairly Valued Before Q1 Print, But Now Attractive
data.description:
Introduction

This is the first time I have covered Bank of America (BAC), after having initiated coverage on JPMorgan (JPM) and Wells Fargo (WFC). However, I usually pay attention to the big banks and their earnings calls because they are often helpful in understanding how the earnings season could unfold.

Bank of America has a one-of-a-kind history, as it was founded in 1904 by the son of Italian immigrants, Amadeo Peter Giannini, as the Bank of Italy in San Francisco to help immigrants and small business owners who were usually not considered by the banks of the early 20th century. In particular, it played a key role after the San Francisco earthquake, when Giannini asked as a "collateral" to lend money just the callous hands of those who asked him for money. He was also known for his sober lifestyle, as he always considered that it was useless to have a net worth above $500,000 (around $6M today, if we adjust it for inflation), as he thought that a man becomes a slave to money when he has too much. Warren Buffett once said that he invested in Bank of America after the GFC because he had read the book "Biography of a Bank" about Giannini and came to admire his way of building the bank.

Being a universal bank, the company has four main segments: Consumer Banking, Global Wealth and Investment Management, Global Banking, and Global Markets. Therefore, its revenues come from two main streams: net interest income and noninterest income, with a 53%-47% split as of FY25.

Key Economic Drivers

I take it almost for granted that most SA readers are aware of what drives and impacts a bank's earnings. However, allow me to quickly recap a few of the most important ones so that we are all aligned. Surely, the largest lever is the Fed's interest rate policy. As I think most of us, by now, are accepting the higher-for-longer outlook, BAC's NII benefits because of how its fixed-rate assets reprice. Rate cuts, on the other hand, compress NII. At the end of FY25, BAC expected one or two 25 bps cuts, but now the outlook has changed, and most investors expect no more than one cut, with some even wondering whether we could see a rate hike rather than another cut. In this situation, BAC's NII is going to be rather strong and consistent for some time.

Clearly, this is also a risk: if interest rates suddenly drop, banks have a problem. However, we should be aware of one important trend. During the zero-rate environment that lasted from 2020 through 2022, financing was extremely convenient. However, these loans (especially the corporate ones) are now hitting their maturity wall, and this means that they will be refinanced at higher rates. This is creating an NII expansion arc that could last for at least three years, if not more. The arc has already been visible since Q1 2025, so my assumption is that it will extend at least through the end of FY27.

Secondly, we have to consider how its loan portfolio grows and credit quality. Loan growth is positive per se, but it could be meaningless if credit quality deteriorates: a bank wants to be as sure as possible that the money it lends will be paid back with interest on top. If credit deteriorates and delinquency rates increase, the bank has to increase its provisions, which reduces its earnings. So, credit quality can be considered as something similar to a swing variable.

A third driver is the activity in the capital markets. If it is high, BAC generates more fees and trading revenue. Usually, when the market is in bull mode, this is a tailwind for the bank, but a bear market is also usually a tailwind. Volatility is always an ally.

Bank of America has several advantages. The first one I think of is its scale, with almost 90M clients and $2T in average deposits. We know that it has the second-lowest-cost funding base after JPMorgan. Being a universal bank, it also tries to move its consumer banking clients to Global Wealth and Investment Management or Global Markets products, so that it can generate incremental revenues at almost no marginal cost.

Bank of America's Q1 2026 Earnings Review

Bank of America reported strong earnings, especially when compared to Wells Fargo's (WFC), which I just discussed. The bank, as shown below, reported net income growth of 17% to $8.6B, with its EPS up 25% to $1.11, and an efficiency ratio that has moved rapidly down from 63% a few quarters ago to 61%. This matters because it creates operating leverage: as we can see, BAC's revenues grew by 7%, which means that each dollar of additional revenue has generated higher earnings. The bank's balance sheet is strong, with deposits up 3% to $2T and loans up 9% to $1.2T. The CET1 ratio is also well above the regulatory minimum of 8.5% and stands at 11.2%.

Wells Fargo, on the other hand, reported EPS growth of 15% and a ROTCE of 14.5%, while Bank of America is up 203 bps to 16%. Its efficiency ratio of 67% is significantly worse than Bank of America's.

As we can see below, all four main segments reported strong growth. As predicted, GWIM was the strongest one, but I think that seeing positive growth in global banking and global markets is also a sign of resilience. But we have to outline how consumer banking did well and, therefore, the quarterly NII of $15.7B came ahead of the prior guidance of $15.4B. Here, Bank of America shows that it is already benefiting from fixed-rate asset repricing, something that Wells Fargo failed to report in Q1 2026.

Credit quality was also important, as the bank reduced its YoY provisions to $1.3B (Q1 2025: $1.5B), with the NCO ratio improving by 6 bps YoY to 0.48%. Nonperforming loans were also stable at around $5.8B, the same as in Q4 and lower than the Q1 2025 print of $6.1B.

BAC returned $9.3B to its shareholders during the quarter, $2.1B of which was paid in dividends, and another $7.2B returned via a buyback. If we annualize this, we are talking about roughly $37B returned, which would be 24% above the $30B returned in FY2025. This could lead to a sharecount reduction of 3-4%, depending on the price of the stock. Considering the current market cap of $381B, we are talking about a yield above 9%, while the 10-yr Treasury yields 4.3%.

Valuation

As I was preparing for the print, I considered Bank of America more or less fairly valued, but the report is above my expectations and changed my assumptions in two ways: ROTCE has been high for several quarters, and NII guidance seems to be a bit conservative, given that the repricing has not concluded yet. Moreover, the comparison with WFC shows a divergence between the two banks, with BAC actually pulling away. By the way, a few months ago, we talked about AI being a structural tailwind for financials, as it would have made them flex their operating leverage. Bank of America is showing this already.

Now, let's come to the bank's valuation. My base case is now revised, and I expect the bank to report FY EPS of $5.00. At a multiple of 11.5, we have a price target of $57.50. The bear case is built on $4.25 EPS with a 10 multiple, which would lead to a valuation of $42.50. The bull case considers FY25 EPS of $5.25 with a multiple of 13x, which would give a price target of $68.25. When I use a 20% probability for the bear case, a 55% probability for the base case, and a 25% probability for the bull case, the final price is $57.20, which leaves around $3 of upside compared to the current price.

My conviction moves from neutral to positive, although I would consider BAC only for a starter position at the current levels. We have a bit of upside, and the outlook is positive, but we also have to admit that we are not far away from my intrinsic value calculation. This means that BAC is a buy, but that it could be interesting to start a position to monitor the stock and then pull the trigger on any pullback.
